summary: I was born in Middletown, Ohio, where my parents moved for work after my dad returned from the Army. My dad worked at Armco Steel there, and we lived in Middletown until I was 13. It was a place full of bluegrass musicians who would come to our house and play all night, which deeply influenced my love for music.

My mom is from Bath County, Kentucky, and after living in Ohio, my family moved back there when I was 13. I attended and graduated from Bath County High School, where I was involved in the Radio Club. Bath County has always felt like home, and it’s where I reconnected with my roots and community.

Preston, Kentucky, is where my mother is originally from and where my dad lived after retirement. When my dad wasn’t well, I knew I needed to come back home to Preston to be with my family. It’s a small place that holds a lot of family history and memories for me.

My dad was from Menifee County, Kentucky, and after his service in the Army, he met my mom and eventually moved to Ohio for work. Menifee County is part of my family heritage, and even though I was born in Ohio, my roots are deeply connected to that area.

My dad served in the United States Army before he met my mom. His time in the Army was a significant chapter in his life, and after returning, he settled down and started a family. His military service shaped part of our family story and influenced where we lived afterward.

After my dad returned from the Army, he worked at Armco Steel in Middletown, Ohio, because that was where the jobs were. Armco Steel was a big part of our life in Ohio, and many people from Eastern Kentucky, like my dad’s friends, worked there too. It was a hub for folks from our region who had moved north for work.

I graduated from Morehead State University in December 1991 with a degree in Radio, Television, and Public Relations Advertising. It was a great foundation for my career in media and marketing. Morehead State helped me prepare for my first jobs in Lexington and beyond.

After college, I worked in Lexington selling TV commercials for what is now Spectrum but was called TCI back then. I spent about six years there before moving into radio and later real estate. Lexington was an important place for my early career and professional growth.

While working in Lexington for the cable company, I sold TV commercials for networks like ESPN. It was part of my job to connect local businesses with national channels, which gave me great experience in advertising and media sales. ESPN was one of the big names I worked with during that time.

CNN was another major network I sold advertising for while working in Lexington at the cable company. Being involved with big networks like CNN helped me understand the media landscape and prepared me for future roles in radio and marketing. It was an exciting time early in my career.

The cable company I worked for in Lexington is now known as Spectrum, but back then it was called TCI. I sold TV commercials for them, including for networks like ESPN and CNN. Spectrum represents the evolution of the company I worked for early in my career.

TCI was the name of the cable company I worked for in Lexington before it became Spectrum. I sold TV commercials there starting in the early 1990s, which was my first job after college. TCI gave me valuable experience in media sales and marketing.

In the summer of 1992, I moved to Dallas, Texas, for a position with the cable company working in the TV market there. Although it was a great opportunity, I quickly realized I was homesick for Kentucky’s landscape and community. After about a year, I returned home thanks to my dad’s encouragement.

I worked in radio for what is now called iHeartRadio, though during my time the company changed ownership several times. Radio has always been my passion, and working there allowed me to combine my love of music and media. It’s where I found my true calling after years in TV advertising and real estate.

When I decided to come back home for good, I bought a house on High Street in Mount Sterling. It’s where I live now and where I work as the Tourism Director and at the local radio station WMST. Mount Sterling is a close-knit community that I’m proud to be part of.

I started working at WMST radio in Mount Sterling after returning to Kentucky, initially just to pay the mortgage. I’ve been there almost 17 years now, doing morning shows, weather, commercials, and news. WMST has been a great place to stay connected to my passion for radio and the community.

Paul Mullins is a famous bluegrass musician I grew up around in Middletown, Ohio. My dad would have bluegrass musicians like Paul come to our house and play music all night, which deeply influenced my love for music. Knowing Paul and others like him helped shape my musical background.

Tom T. Hall was a name often mentioned around my family and friends because of his connection to the bluegrass and country music scene. While I don’t recall if he ever came to our house, he was definitely part of the musical culture I grew up with. Talking about him was common among the musicians in our circle.

Owingsville has a radio station, but when I was in high school at Bath County, we would come to WMST in Mount Sterling for our Radio Club shows. Although Owingsville had its own station, WMST was the hub for local radio activities that I participated in as a teenager.

Dayton, Ohio, is near where I was born in Middletown, and there’s a strong cultural connection between Eastern Kentucky and that area. Many people from Eastern Kentucky, including my family and friends, moved to places like Dayton for work. It’s an easy drive from there to Kentucky, which helps maintain ties between the regions.
